analogy: Think of "Tissues" through the chapter's own opening images: How Forces Affect Motion Chapter 6 In Chapter 4, you learnt to describe the motion of an object in terms of its position, velocity and acceleration. Is there an underlying cause for a change in position and velocity of an object? In this chapter, we... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 6.1 The Concept of Force, 6.1.1 Measuring the magnitude of a force, 6.2 Balanced and Unbalanced Forces, 96 Exploration|Grade 9. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Tissues is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that how Forces Affect Motion Chapter 6 In Chapter 4, you learnt to describe the motion of an object in terms of its position, velocity and acceleration.

The text explains that is there an underlying cause for a change in position and velocity of an object?

The text explains that in this chapter, we will investigate what causes changes in the motion of objects.

The text explains that we will also discuss Newton’s three laws of motion and learn how to apply them.

The text explains that 6.1 The Concept of Force You may recall learning earlier that a force can make an object move from rest, change the speed and direction of motion of a moving object, and can even change the shape of an object.

The text explains that for example, a ball at rest starts moving when you apply a force, a force applied by a cricket bat on a cricket ball changes its direction, and a lemon can be squeezed by the force applied by your fingers (Fig.

The text explains that 6.1: Applying force on different objects (b) Striking a ball (a) Kicking a ball Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 5 Think It Over y Why does a canoe move forward when the canoeist pushes water backwards with their paddle and why does it move faster when they push harder? y Suppose the same canoeist uses the same paddle force in two different canoes, one empty and one carrying another passenger.

The text explains that 6.3: (a) Pushing a box kept on table or floor Force by hand Force of friction Fig.

The text explains that 6.2: Measuring the weight of an object using a spring balance While learning about force earlier, did you notice that whenever any type of force acting on an object was described, its direction was also specified?

The text explains that for example, the phrases used were, force of friction acting on an object in a direction opposite to the direction of its motion; like poles of a magnet repelling each other or unlike poles attracting each other due to the magnetic force; like charges repelling or unlike charges attracting each other due to the electrostatic force; the Earth attracting objects towards itself due to the gravitational force; buoyant force exerted by liquid in an upward direction on an object placed in it, and so on.

The text explains that force is a physical quantity for which we need to specify direction along with its magnitude and unit, just like for the physical quantities — position, displacement, velocity and acceleration of an object which were introduced in Chapter 4.

The text explains that the SI unit of force is the newton (written with a small ‘n’) and its symbol is N.

The text explains that the magnitude of the force expresses its strength.

The text explains that if either the magnitude or direction, or the both, of a force applied on an object changes, the effect of the force also changes.

The text explains that note 6.1.1 Measuring the magnitude of a force How can we measure the magnitude of a force?

The text explains that do you remember using a spring balance earlier to measure the weight of objects (Fig.

The text explains that do you also remember that the weight of an object is the gravitational force with which the Earth pulls the object?

The text explains that in everyday life, the smallest forces we can directly feel are of the order of millinewtons (10–3 N), such as a light touch.

The text explains that for example, when you are pushing a box placed on a surface then, apart from the force with which you are pushing it, the force of friction is also acting on the box in the direction opposite to that of the motion (Fig.

The text explains that two forces are acting upon it — gravitational force by the Earth acting downwards and buoyant force applied by the liquid acting upwards.

The text explains that have you ever played a game of tug of war where two teams pull at a rope in opposite directions?

The text explains that if both the teams pull the rope with equal Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 5 Fig.

The text explains that 6.3: (b) A ball floating on water Buoyant force Grade 6 Curiosity Chapter 4 Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 5 Gravitational force

The text explains that such two forces, which are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction are called balanced forces.

The text explains that however, if one team pulls harder, i.e., it applies a force of larger magnitude, the forces are no longer balanced and the rope moves in the direction of the larger force (Fig.

The text explains that when two forces are opposite in direction but unequal in magnitude (Fig.

The text explains that 6.4b), the magnitude of net force is equal to the difference between the magnitudes of two forces and the direction is along the force of the larger magnitude.

The text explains that now, think of a situation where two people are applying forces in the same direction on a stalled car to make it move (Fig.

The text explains that the direction of the net force is in the same direction as the two individual forces.

The text explains that what is the magnitude and the direction of the net force acting on the block in each case?

The text explains that 6.6: Two forces acting on a block in three different manner 10 N 6 N (a) 10 N 6 N (b) 10 N 6 N (c) Answer: (a) Net force = 10 N + 6 N = 16 N, acting towards the right side.

The text explains that (b) Net force = 10 N – 6 N = 4 N, acting towards the right side.

The text explains that 6.4: Two forces applied in opposite direction of (a) equal magnitudes, (b) unequal magnitudes (b) Net force Fig.

The text explains that 6.5: Two forces applied in the same direction Net force

The text explains that suppose an object is kept at rest on the floor and you apply a force on it in the forward direction (Fig.

The text explains that it is due to the force of friction arising between the bottom surface of the box and the floor acting in a direction opposite to the direction of the force applied by you.

The text explains that the box will start moving when the force applied by you is of larger magnitude than the force of friction, so that a net force acts on the box in the direction of its motion.

The text explains that grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 5 For an object being pushed, apart from the applied force and the force of friction, some other forces may also be acting on it (Fig.

The text explains that the weight acts in the downwards direction, whereas the normal force acts in the upward direction perpendicular to the surface.

The text explains that at the instant, when the arms tilt to the front direction (out of the page towards you), are the forces exerted by the players balanced?

The text explains that otherwise, the force of friction acting against the direction of motion brings the object to rest.

The text explains that upon release, the force applied by you Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 5 What if ... the force of friction disappears in the world?

The text explains that 6.13: Force acting on the stack of coins (a) due to the stretched rubber band and friction upon release of rubber band, and (b) only due to friction (a) (b) Force of friction B B Force of friction Force due to rubber band vanishes and the force applied by the stretched rubber band in the forward direction on the stack of coins sets it moving.

The text explains that attach a heavy bucket to one end and try to hold it by the other end (Fig.

The text explains that now, add one more turn of the rope and repeat.

The text explains that you will find that each extra turn increases the ‘grip’ between the rope and the branch, increasing the friction and reducing the force required, making it much easier to hold the same load.

The text explains that the reduction in effort is much larger than you might expect from just adding one turn.

The text explains that this shows that friction does not increase in a simple linear way, small changes in contact can lead to large changes in force.

The text explains that in the same way, friction between a rope and a post allows large ships to be held safely at a pier. y It is often instructive to examine how scientific ideas develop over time.

The text explains that if you are interested, explore how Newton formulated the laws of motion by reading excerpts from his original work, the Principia.

The text explains that both the original text and commentaries are available online.

The text explains that newton’s laws describe motion across an enormous range of scales, from everyday objects to planets and stars.

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 6: Tissues

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The forces always occur in pairs but remember that these two forces act on two different objects. (NCERT Class 9 Science Ch6 pg 14)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The two forces are equal in magnitude but act on different objects, the paddle and the water, so they do not cancel each other. (NCERT Class 9 Science Ch6 pg 14)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 6.1 The Concept of Force and 6.1.1 Measuring the magnitude of a force as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Tissues; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Tissues through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 6.1 The Concept of Force and 6.1.1 Measuring the magnitude of a force to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
